title: Erratum to “The Impact of Corporate Sustainability on Organizational Processes and Performance”

author.Robert G. Eccles: Saïd Business School, University of Oxford, Oxford OX1 1HP, United Kingdom
author.Ioannis Ioannou: Strategy and Entrepreneurship, London Business School, London NW1 4SA, United Kingdom
author.George Serafeim: Harvard Business School, Harvard Univeristy, Boston, Massachusetts 02163

article.Correction 1: On page 2849, regarding the comparison in performance between high- and low-sustainability companies, there was a typo in a sentence inside the parenthesis. For the equal-weighted portfolio analysis, the difference in alphas between the low- and high-sustainability portfolios isnotstatistically significant. Therefore, the text within the parentheses should read, “(not significant at the 10% level or below).” Reported estimated coefficients andp-values in Table 7 are not affected.
article.Correction 2: We identified a coding error related to using end-of-month rather than beginning-of-month market equity values for the value-weighted portfolio returns reported in Table 7 of the paper (p. 2849). This causes both the low- and high-sustainability portfolios to exhibit better performance than when beginning-of-month market value of equity is used, given that end-of-month weighting would increase the weight on stocks with better monthly returns. When we value-weight the portfolios with beginning-of-month market capitalization the alphas on both low and high sustainability decrease. As we discuss in the paper, given that both the high- and low-sustainability samples include, by design, surviving, financially strong firms, the focus cannot be on the absolute performance of each portfolio, but rather on the difference in performance across the high- and low-sustainability portfolios. The high-sustainability portfolio still exhibits a higher alpha than the low-sustainability portfolio, as in the original paper. The difference between the alphas is 4.4% (originally reported as 4.8% in the published paper) and it is statistically significant at the 5% level (originally reported also as statistically significant at the 5% level in the published paper). The corrected columns of Table 7 are as follows: Table We apologize for any confusion or inconvenience these errors may have caused to the readers.
